text.title: Training Hyperparameters and GPU Selection
text.summary: Hyperparameter ranges, learning rate schedule selection, gradient accumulation pattern, GPU VRAM requirements by model size, and cloud GPU cost table.

rule.statement: Start with learning_rate=2e-4, batch_size=2, gradient_accumulation=4, epochs=3, cosine scheduler — adjust only after observing validation loss curves.
rule.rationale: These defaults produce stable training on 7B models with LoRA. Starting with defaults and adjusting from observed loss behavior is faster than grid-searching from scratch.

list.item: learning_rate: 1e-5 to 5e-4 for LoRA; lower (1e-6 to 5e-6) for DPO/ORPO
batch_size: 1-8 per GPU, effective batch = batch * grad_accum * n_gpus
epochs: 1-5; 3 is a safe default; increase if training loss is still decreasing at epoch 3
warmup_ratio: 0.03-0.1 — prevents loss spikes in early training
weight_decay: 0.01-0.1 — light regularization; higher for overfitting risk
max_seq_length: 512-8192 — set to 95th percentile of dataset sequence length to avoid padding waste

rule.statement: Use bfloat16 on A100/H100 instead of float16 — avoids NaN loss without quality loss; use float16 only on older GPU architectures that do not support bf16.
rule.rationale: bfloat16 has a wider exponent range than float16, preventing gradient underflow/overflow that causes NaN loss on long training runs.

rule.statement: Effective batch size = per_device_batch * gradient_accumulation * num_gpus; target effective batch 8-32 for stable training.
rule.rationale: Too small effective batch causes noisy gradients; too large reduces generalization. Gradient accumulation achieves large effective batch on limited VRAM.
